name: Total Bilirubin
summary: Bilirubin is a yellow pigment produced when the body breaks down old red blood cells; the liver processes it and excretes it in bile. Total bilirubin measures both the unconjugated (indirect) and conjugated (direct) forms in blood.
whatItMeans: An elevated total bilirubin can reflect either increased red-cell breakdown (hemolysis, raising indirect bilirubin) or reduced liver clearance/bile flow (liver disease or bile-duct obstruction, raising direct bilirubin). Fractionating into direct vs. indirect helps localize the cause; a benign inherited pattern (Gilbert syndrome) raises indirect bilirubin without true liver disease. Visible jaundice typically appears once total bilirubin exceeds roughly 2-3 mg/dL. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Hemolysis / increased red-cell breakdown (raises indirect bilirubin)
Gilbert syndrome (benign, raises indirect bilirubin, often with fasting or stress)
lowers: Resolution of the underlying liver, biliary, or hemolytic condition
confounders: Fasting and prolonged starvation can raise indirect bilirubin (notably in Gilbert syndrome)
Hemolyzed sample can affect measured values
Direct vs. indirect fractionation is needed to interpret an elevation
Exposure of the sample to light can falsely lower bilirubin

ranges.note: General adult reference range for total bilirubin; varies slightly by lab.
